1: The superscription marks this as a new oracle unit — the davar YHWH (word of YHWH) formula distinguishes commissioned prophetic speech from the prophet's own words. The MKT renders it plainly as "This is the word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD" to emphasize the divine commissioning rather than Jeremiah's own initiative.
2: ʿamad bĕšaʿar bêt YHWH — "stand at the gate of the LORD's house"; the temple gate was the highest-traffic public space in Jerusalem, making this a maximally public proclamation. The MKT follows MT in specifying "all you of Judah who come through these gates to worship" — the sermon is addressed to the worshipping public, not just the priesthood.
3: hêtîbû darkêkem — "reform your ways and your actions"; the Hebrew heitev (make good, reform) is an intensive hiphil: genuine transformation, not superficial adjustment. The MKT's "reform your ways and your actions" preserves the dual emphasis — both conduct (derekh, path/lifestyle) and deeds (maʿăśîm, concrete acts). The conditional structure follows: reform → remain in the land.
4: divrê hashsheqer — "deceptive words"; sheqer is falsehood, deception, fraud — the same root used for false oaths and false prophecy. The triple repetition "the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD!" mimics what the crowd was saying and ridicules it simultaneously. The MKT preserves the triple repetition because it is structurally essential to understanding what YHWH is calling false.
5: The conditional clause of the temple-sermon: kî ʾim heytîb teyṭîbû — an infinitive absolute construction (literally "if in reforming you truly reform") that the MKT renders "if you truly reform." The infinitive absolute with a finite verb is a Hebrew device for emphasis; "truly" captures this better than a literal translation that would seem redundant.
6: gēr, yātôm, ʾalmānâ — the triad of the vulnerable: foreigner, fatherless, widow. This triad recurs throughout the OT as the paradigmatic category of the socially unprotected. "Shed innocent blood" (dām nāqî) refers to judicial murder; "follow other gods" is the religious-covenantal violation. The MKT preserves all three categories to show the breadth of the required reform.
7: The apodosis of the conditional temple-sermon: wĕšikantî ʾetkem — "I will let you live in this place." Šākan is the verb root of miškan (tabernacle/dwelling); YHWH promises continued dwelling-in-place if covenant obligations are met. The phrase "for ever and ever" (lĕmîn-ʿôlām wĕʿad-ʿôlām) is emphatic — the land-promise is indefinitely open, contingent on faithfulness.
8: divrê hashsheqer lĕvilti hoʿîl — "deceptive words that can do you no good"; lĕvilti hoʿîl is a common prophetic phrase for things that are utterly useless. The MKT renders it idiomatically — the point is that these words not only fail to help but actively harm those who trust in them. The temple-security slogan is not merely inert but destructive.
9: The covenant-violation catalogue: steal (gānōb), murder (rāṣaḥ), commit adultery (nāʾap), swear falsely (šābaʿ laššeqer), sacrifice to Baal, follow other gods. This is a near-echo of the Decalogue violations — six of the Ten Commandments broken by the worshipping community. The MKT preserves the staccato list to convey the cumulative effect of itemized covenant-breaking.
10: nilṣalnû — "we are safe"; from nāṣal, to be rescued/delivered. The crowd enters the temple guilty of Decalogue violations and immediately declares themselves delivered. The MKT's "We are safe!" captures the presumptuous self-assurance better than "we are delivered" — it is not a theological claim but a smug dismissal of the need for repentance.
11: mĕʿārat pārîṣîm — "den of robbers"; pārîṣîm are violent men, robbers, but specifically violent ones who use force (pāraṣ, to break through). This verse is directly cited by Jesus in the Temple Cleansing (Matt 21:13; Mark 11:17; Luke 19:46). The MKT follows the traditional rendering because it is the phrase preserved in the NT citation; "den of violent robbers" would be technically more precise but would obscure the biblical cross-reference.
12: Šîlōh — Shiloh was the first permanent sanctuary site of the tabernacle (Josh 18:1), destroyed by the Philistines ca. 1050 BCE (1 Sam 4). YHWH cites his own destruction of Shiloh as precedent for what he can and will do to Jerusalem. The MKT renders "my place that was in Shiloh" to preserve the possessive — YHWH acknowledges Shiloh was his; that did not protect it.
13: haškēm wĕdabbēr — "I spoke again and again"; the idiomatic Hebrew for speaking persistently and early (hiškim = to rise early, hence to act persistently). The MKT renders it "I spoke to you again and again" rather than the more literal "rising early to speak" because the idiom's force is persistency, not the time of day.
14: The threat parallels the Shiloh precedent: as Shiloh was destroyed, so will this temple. The phrase ʾăšer nātattî lākem wĕlaʾăbôtêkem — "that I gave to you and your ancestors" — underlines that the gift of the temple does not make it inviolable; what YHWH gives, YHWH can take. The MKT preserves this emphasis by making "gave" explicit.
15: kol-zeraʿ ʾeprāyim — "the entire offspring of Ephraim"; the Northern Kingdom was "driven out" (gārašti) in the Assyrian deportation of 722 BCE. YHWH here says explicitly that he drove them out from his presence — the passive phrasing common in history-writing is corrected to active divine agency. The MKT preserves "I drove out" to maintain the theological claim.
16: ʾal-tittpallēl... wĕʾal-tippgaʿ-bî — "do not pray... do not press me with intercession"; this is one of the most startling commands in the prophets: YHWH forbids Jeremiah to intercede. Pāgaʿ means to encounter, meet, intercede; the MKT's "do not press me" captures the picture of Jeremiah's characteristic intercessory intensity. The prohibition signals that judgment has become irrevocable.
17: A rhetorical question directed at Jeremiah: "Do you not see?" (hărōʾeh ʾattā). YHWH points Jeremiah to the visible idolatry in public spaces as evidence for the judgment he has just pronounced. The MKT's "towns of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem" follows MT in specifying both urban and rural scope of the apostate worship.
18: meleʾket haššāmayim — "queen of heaven"; the Hebrew phrase (with meleʾket = work/labor/queen, a disputed reading) refers to the Mesopotamian goddess Ishtar/Inanna or the Canaanite Asherah. The MKT renders "queen of heaven" — the majority reading that fits the context of family-organized cultic baking of cakes in the goddess's image.
19: YHWH's rhetorical response: is it me they are provoking (hăkʿăsunî)? The answer is no — the idolaters only harm themselves. The reflexive dimension of sin is captured in "bringing shame on themselves" (lĕmāʿan bōšet pĕnêhem). The MKT preserves this by asking "Is it not themselves they are provoking?" — YHWH is not threatened by idol-worship; the worshippers are self-destructing.
20: ʾap YHWH wĕḥămātî — "My anger and my wrath"; the pairing of ʾap (nose/anger, the nose flares in anger) and ḥēmâ (burning wrath, heat) intensifies the divine emotion beyond ordinary anger. The MKT renders both: "my anger and my wrath." The judgment is on humans, animals, trees, and ground — a reversal of creation's fruitfulness.
21: sĕpû ʿôlôtêkem ʿal-zivḥêkem — "add your burnt offerings to your other sacrifices and eat the meat"; this is ironic permission: since you treat the temple as a guarantor of safety regardless of behavior, go ahead and eat the burnt offerings (normally reserved entirely for God). The MKT preserves the sarcasm by rendering "Go ahead — add your burnt offerings."
22: lō-dibbartî... ʿal-ʾōlôt — "I did not speak with them... about burnt offerings and sacrifices"; this difficult verse argues that the Exodus command's prime content was covenantal relationship and obedience, not sacrificial detail. The MKT follows the MT: "I did not speak or give them commands about burnt offerings." The emphasis is not that the sacrificial law was never given, but that it was subordinate to the covenantal relationship. Verse 23 immediately supplies what the prime command actually was.
23: šimʿû bĕqôlî — "Obey my voice" or "listen to my voice"; the MKT renders "Obey me" — the prime command given at Sinai is not a list of sacrifices but a relational summons to hearing-obedience. "I will be your God, you will be my people" (lĕʿam / lēʾlōhîm) is the covenant formula; "walk in all the ways I command you" follows from the relational priority.
24: bĕsĕrîrût libbām hārāʿ — "the stubbornness of their evil hearts"; šĕrîrût denotes hardness, stubbornness — the intractable resistance of the will. This phrase recurs as one of Jeremiah's signature diagnostic terms for Israel's condition. The MKT translates "the stubbornness of their evil hearts" rather than "the imagination of their evil hearts" (KJV) because šĕrîrût is primarily about hardness/stubbornness, not imagination.
25: haškēm wĕšālôaḥ — "persistently sent"; another idiom using the "rising early" root for persistent action. The MKT renders "I have persistently sent all my servants the prophets" — the emphasis is on the relentless, patient divine effort to communicate before judgment. The phrase "again and again" (repeated from v.13) underlines the long record of ignored warnings.
26: qaššû ʾet-ʿarpām — "they were stubborn and stiff-necked"; qāšâ ʿōrep (hard of neck) is the classic stiff-necked idiom for resistance to divine correction. The MKT renders both the "stubborn" and "stiff-necked" (rather than collapsing them into one English word) to preserve the rhetorical doubling. "They behaved even worse than their ancestors" — the downward spiral of apostasy through the generations.
27: YHWH addresses Jeremiah directly: you will preach and they will not listen — a pre-announcement of failure. This functions as prophetic call-within-call: Jeremiah is told in advance that his ministry will be formally rejected. The MKT preserves the direct address ("You will speak... you will call") to maintain the personal commissioning of the prophet.
28: ʾemûnâ ʾābĕdâ — "Truth has vanished"; ʾĕmûnâ is faithfulness, reliability, truth as a practiced commitment (not merely propositional accuracy). The loss of ʾĕmûnâ is the social-ethical collapse of covenant. The MKT renders "Truth has vanished from their lips" — ʾĕmûnâ is more than truth-telling; it is the fabric of communal trustworthiness that covenant loyalty creates.
29: gĕzî nizrēk — "Shave off your hair and throw it away"; cutting off one's hair (nēzer = consecrated hair/crown, here in the sense of hair as one's "crown") as a mourning gesture. The MKT renders "Shave off your hair" — the mourning rite signals a lament over the generation YHWH has rejected and abandoned.
30: gillûlêhem — "their detestable idols"; gillûlîm is a contemptuous term for idols (possibly related to the word for dung-pellets or logs). The MKT uses "detestable idols" — the term is deliberately derogatory, YHWH refusing to name the idols with their own titles. Installing them babbayit ʾăšer-niqrāʾ šĕmî ʿālāw (in the house on which my name is called) is the ultimate desecration.
31: lō-tsiwwîtî — "something I never commanded"; the explicit divine repudiation of child sacrifice. Bāmôt hattōpet bĕgêʾ ben-hinnōm — the shrines of Topheth in the valley of Ben Hinnom (the geographic origin of Gehenna). YHWH states clearly that child sacrifice was never in the covenant: "something I never commanded, nor did it enter my mind." The MKT preserves the first-person emphatic (lō-tsiwwîtî) to make the repudiation direct and unambiguous.
32: gêʾ hahărēgâ — "Valley of Slaughter"; the Valley of Ben Hinnom where children were burned will be renamed. The MKT preserves the ominous name "Valley of Slaughter" — Topheth (place of burning) becomes a mass burial site. This verse begins the judgment-description that the temple sermon has been building toward.
33: hayyôm lāzāyit — "food for the birds of the sky and the wild animals"; unburied corpses are the ultimate covenant curse (Deut 28:26). The phrase "no one will drive them away" means even the minimal dignity of shooing away scavengers will be absent. The MKT follows MT precisely here because the covenant-curse language needs no softening.
34: qôl śāśôn wĕqôl śimḥâ qôl ḥātān wĕqôl kallâ — "the sounds of joy and gladness, the voices of the bridegroom and the bride"; the four-fold joy-formula that will be silenced. This phrase also appears in 16:9; 25:10; 33:11 (where its restoration signals new-covenant renewal). The MKT preserves all four terms: joy, gladness, bridegroom, bride — the complete vocabulary of wedding celebration ended.
